summary: I served primarily during the Vietnam War around 1971 and 1972. My time there was tough; it was hot and humid, and I lost about 30 to 35 pounds in two months. Despite the hardships, I appreciated the beauty of the country, especially the orchids and their scent. I was able to keep in touch with my family through the Military Auxiliary Radio System, which was a real blessing during those times.

I enlisted voluntarily in the United States Navy on January 19, 1954, when I was just 17 years old. I trained at San Diego and served on a brand new minesweeper commissioned in Tacoma, Washington in 1955. I also spent time at Alameda Naval Air Station and Long Beach, California, which was my home port in the States. Later, I was stationed overseas at Sasebo Naval Base in Japan, where I traveled extensively throughout Southeast Asia before leaving the Navy in June 1957.

San Diego was where I went for my Navy basic training back in 1954. It was the starting point of my military career before I moved on to other bases like Alameda Naval Air Station and Tacoma. San Diego was important to me as the place where I first learned the skills that would carry me through my service.

After basic training in San Diego, I was stationed at Alameda Naval Air Station. From there, I was sent to Tacoma, Washington to commission a new minesweeper. Alameda was a key step in my early Navy career, bridging my initial training and my first sea duty.

In Tacoma, Washington, I helped put a brand new minesweeper into commission on October 16, 1955. That was a proud moment for me during my Navy service. Tacoma was a significant place because it was where I first saw a ship come to life under my watch.

Long Beach, California was my home port while I was in the Navy. After my time in Tacoma, I returned there before heading overseas. It felt like home and was an important place to me during my early military years.

I was stationed at Sasebo Naval Base in Japan during my Navy service overseas. From there, I traveled all over Southeast Asia, including the Marietas Islands and the Marshall Islands. Sasebo was my home base while I was abroad, and it was a hub for many of my deployments.

While stationed at Sasebo Naval Base, I sailed all over Southeast Asia. It was a vast area that I got to see during my Navy service in the mid-1950s. Traveling through those waters gave me a broader perspective of the region during that time.

During my Navy time overseas, I sailed up and down the Marietas Islands. It was part of my travels while based out of Sasebo Naval Base in Japan. Those islands were among the many places I visited in Southeast Asia.

I also sailed to the Marshall Islands during my Navy service in the mid-1950s. This was part of my extensive travel throughout the Pacific while stationed overseas. The Marshall Islands were one of the many stops on my journey before returning to the States in 1957.

After leaving the Navy in 1957, I worked for Boeing in Seattle for about a year building airplanes. It was a change from military life, but I stayed connected to aviation through that job. Boeing gave me valuable experience before I joined the Air Force in 1959.

I joined the United States Air Force on February 25, 1959, after my time with Boeing. I trained as a jet engine mechanic and eventually became a superintendent. My Air Force career took me to several bases including Chanute Air Force Base in Illinois and Forbes Air Force Base in Kansas, where I served in Strategic Air Command during the Cuban Missile Crisis.

Rantoul, Illinois was where Chanute Air Force Base is located, and that's where I attended jet engine school after joining the Air Force. It was an important training period for me to become a jet engine technician. My time there prepared me for the technical roles I held later in my career.

Chanute Air Force Base in Rantoul, Illinois was where I went to jet engine school after enlisting in the Air Force. It was the place where I learned the skills that defined my Air Force career. The training there was tough but essential for my later work as a jet engine mechanic and superintendent.

I spent nearly five years at Forbes Air Force Base in Topeka, Kansas, serving in the Strategic Air Command. This was during the early 1960s, including the tense period of the Cuban Missile Crisis in 1962. Forbes was where I flew RB-47 reconnaissance missions over Cuba, often under very demanding conditions.

Topeka, Kansas was home to Forbes Air Force Base where I served for nearly five years in the Strategic Air Command. It was a critical location during the Cuban Missile Crisis when we flew reconnaissance missions over Cuba. My time in Topeka was intense but vital to national security during that period.

I was part of the Strategic Air Command for nearly five years at Forbes Air Force Base. During the Cuban Missile Crisis in 1962, my unit flew RB-47 reconnaissance bombers over Cuba every hour, 24/7. It was a high-pressure environment where we lost three airplanes and eleven men, and the work was exhausting but crucial.

During the Cuban Missile Crisis in 1962, I was stationed at Forbes Air Force Base with the Strategic Air Command. We flew RB-47 reconnaissance bombers over Cuba every hour, day and night, to gather intelligence. It was a tense time; we lost three airplanes and eleven men, and the work was relentless with shifts often lasting 20 hours or more.

The RB-47 Stratojet was the reconnaissance bomber I flew during the Cuban Missile Crisis. It was equipped with four super cameras in the belly that could take incredibly detailed photos from 40,000 feet. Our unit flew one of these planes every hour over Cuba, and they were loaded with over a hundred million dollars of electronic countermeasures gear when I was later stationed in Turkey.

In 1963, I was stationed at Incirlik Air Base in Turkey for a six-month tour. This was a critical location right across the Dardanelles from Russia during the Cold War. We flew special RB-47s loaded with advanced electronic countermeasures, and the base had top priority for quick launches during any border incidents.

Turkey was where I was stationed at Incirlik Air Base in 1963. It was a strategic location near the Dardanelles and close to Russia during the Cold War. Our missions there involved flying RB-47 reconnaissance aircraft with advanced electronic countermeasures, and we had to be ready to launch within 15 minutes if needed.

I was stationed at Incirlik Air Base in Turkey when President John F. Kennedy was assassinated in 1963. His death was a significant and somber moment during my service there. It was a time of heightened alert and special duties for our unit flying reconnaissance missions.

The Dardanelles Strait was just across from Incirlik Air Base in Turkey where I was stationed in 1963. This narrow waterway was a strategic point near Russia during the Cold War, and our RB-47 flights often operated in this sensitive area. The proximity to the Dardanelles made our missions especially critical.

I retired as a Master Sergeant from the United States Air Force on May 31, 1976. Throughout my career, I worked my way up from a jet engine mechanic to a superintendent and held that rank with pride. It marked the culmination of over two decades of dedicated military service.

My family was stationed near Offutt Air Force Base at one point, and I left them there when I took leave in 1974. I traveled back and forth from there to Thailand by hitchhiking on jet airplanes. Offutt was a place connected to my later Air Force career and family life.

The Military Auxiliary Radio System, or MARS, was how I kept in touch with my family while deployed overseas. It worked like a ham radio system, allowing me to make short calls home without the high cost of long-distance phone calls. I used MARS to speak with my wife from places like Vietnam, Thailand, and Taiwan, which was a real blessing during my service.

I was stationed in Thailand during my Air Force career and was able to communicate with my wife through the Military Auxiliary Radio System while there. I also took leave in 1974 and traveled back and forth between Thailand and the States by hitchhiking on jet airplanes. Thailand was one of the many overseas locations I served at during my military career.

Taiwan was another place I was stationed during my service, and like in Thailand and Vietnam, I used the Military Auxiliary Radio System to keep in touch with my wife. It was important to maintain that connection despite being so far from home. Taiwan was part of the global scope of my military career.
